meta.title: 29th Bulletin of the Grande Armée (Molodetchna — retreat)

lexical_units.contextual_meaning: fortune is treated as smiling or as a force whose chances test soldiers
lexical_units.basic_meaning: luck, fate, or fortune as an abstract condition
lexical_units.basic_meaning_source: Littre / Dictionnaire de l'Academie francaise, historical sense
lexical_units.contrast_explanation: Fortune does not literally smile or act like a human force.
lexical_units.comparison_basis: Campaign outcome is understood through personified fortune.
lexical_units.review_notes: Retain French `fortune` because English fortune/luck/fate split the source-domain range. Gallica OCR noise is visible in the local sentence; use the French span_text as reviewed source text and verify before quotation.
lexical_units.gloss_notes: French `fortune` can mean luck, fate, or fortune; mapping should preserve the range.
French `gloire` carries military fame, radiance, public renown, and honor; do not flatten it into generic English glory.
